mission.title: Install and Configure MySQL Operator for Kubernetes (Oracle)
mission.description: The MySQL Operator for Kubernetes automates the management of MySQL InnoDB Cluster setups within a Kubernetes environment. It simplifies tasks such as deployment, scaling, and upgrades, making it ideal for teams looking to manage MySQL databases efficiently. Key trade-offs include the need for familiarity with Kubernetes and potential complexity in configuration.

steps.title: Add the MySQL Operator Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official MySQL Operator Helm repository to your Helm client, allowing you to install the operator easily.

```bash

helm repo add mysql-operator https://mysql.github.io/mysql-operator/

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the MySQL Operator repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the MySQL Operator
steps.description:
This step installs the MySQL Operator in a dedicated namespace, enabling it to manage MySQL clusters.

```bash

helm install mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator --create-namespace

```

> Installs the MySQL Operator with default settings.

steps.title: Create a secret for MySQL root credentials
steps.description:
This step creates a Kubernetes secret that contains the root user credentials required for managing the MySQL InnoDB Cluster.

```bash

kubectl create secret generic mypwds --from-literal=rootUser=root --from-literal=rootHost=% --from-literal=rootPassword: <REDACTED>'

```

> Replace 'sakila' with your desired root password.

steps.title: Deploy a MySQL InnoDB Cluster
steps.description:
This step defines and deploys a MySQL InnoDB Cluster using a custom resource definition (CRD).

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.4'
EOF

```

> This command creates a new InnoDB Cluster named 'mycluster'.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.4'
EOF

steps.title: Verify the MySQL Operator deployment
steps.description:
This step checks if the MySQL Operator is running correctly in the specified namespace.

```bash

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> You should see the operator deployment status as '1/1' ready.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the MySQL Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the MySQL Operator and its associated resources. Be aware that this may lead to data loss if the MySQL clusters are not backed up.

```bash

helm uninstall mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator

kubectl delete namespace mysql-operator

```

> This command uninstalls the MySQL Operator.
> This command deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the MySQL Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your MySQL data. This step will upgrade the MySQL Operator to the latest version.

```bash

helm upgrade mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> This command upgrades the MySQL Operator to the latest version.
> Verify that the deployment is updated successfully.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Error from server (NotFound): deployments.apps "mysql-operator" not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The MySQL Operator deployment may not have been created successfully.

**Fix:**
Check the Helm installation logs for errors and ensure the namespace is correct.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create resource: secret mypwds already exists'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The secret with the specified name already exists in the namespace.

**Fix:**
Use a different name for the secret or delete the existing one with 'kubectl delete secret mypwds'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'The InnoDB Cluster is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The MySQL pods may not be running or are in a crash loop.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name>' to diagnose the issue.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'kubectl apply -f <file>: error validating data: [apiVersion not set, kind not set]'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The YAML file for the InnoDB Cluster is incorrectly formatted.

**Fix:**
Ensure the YAML file has the correct structure and includes 'apiVersion' and 'kind'.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced support for MySQL 8.4, improved backup automation features.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for upgrading from 8.3.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for MySQL 9.0, enhanced monitoring capabilities.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: Ensure to update your InnoDB Cluster configurations to comply with new version requirements.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get pods -n mysql-operator' showing the MySQL Operator pod running with a status of 'Running'.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.4'
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with sufficient resources and the necessary tools installed.
Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary resources.
